2: The sequence that opens David's fall: wayyarʾ ʾiššāh rōḥeṣet — 'he saw a woman bathing.' The three-step sequence of lust-to-sin that follows — seeing, sending, taking — mirrors the Genesis 3:6 pattern: Eve saw (wattarʾ), desired, and took (wattiqaḥ). The verb rāʾāh (to see) is theologically loaded in the David narrative: YHWH looks on the heart while humans look on the outward appearance (1 Sam 16:7 — the same verb root). David's kingship began with YHWH correcting his seeing; it collapses when David's seeing reverts to the human pattern of desire. James 1:14-15 maps the identical sequence: 'each person is tempted when they are lured and enticed by their own desire; then desire when it has conceived gives birth to sin, and sin when it is fully grown brings forth death.' The narrative demonstrates James's abstract principle with biographical precision: the desire conceived in v2 gives birth to adultery in v4, and brings forth the death of Uriah in v15.
15: The irony of the letter Uriah carries is the narrative's theological coup de grâce: wayyiktōḇ bassēper ʾel yôʾāb wayyišlaḥ bəyad ûriyyāh — 'he wrote in the letter to Joab and sent it by the hand of Uriah.' Uriah the Hittite — the Canaanite proselyte who is more loyal to the covenant than the covenant king — carries his own death warrant without knowing it. The compound irony: (1) Uriah's covenant loyalty (refusal to go home while the Ark is in a tent, v11) is precisely the quality that makes him exploitable; (2) David's position of covenantal authority is precisely what enables his crime; (3) the foreigner is the righteous man and the anointed is the criminal. The narrative is a theological inversion of everything the anointing of ch16 established. The phrase wayyišlaḥ bəyad ûriyyāh echoes the earlier wayyišlaḥ dāwiḏ in vv4 and 6 — the same verb of royal sending, now dispatching the loyal soldier to his death. The three-fold use of šālaḥ (to send) in this chapter — send for Bathsheba, send Uriah to Joab, send the letter — maps the progressive deepening of the abuse of royal power.
1: Vayehi litshuvat ha-shanah le-et tzeit ha-melakhim (“in the spring, when kings typically march out to war”). The phrase teshuvat ha-shanah (the turning/return of the year) is the spring agricultural-military season when roads were dry and campaigns were feasible. The convention le-et tzeit ha-melakhim (the time kings go out) identifies this as the normal season for royal military leadership. David sends Joab with the fighting forces — but David yashuv biYrushalayim (“David himself stayed in Jerusalem”). The narrative’s first word of moral evaluation is structural: the king who should be at war is instead at home. The Ammonite war has been ongoing; the choice to remain is David’s decision, not necessity.
3: David sent someone to investigate and was told: halo-zot Bat-Sheva bat-Eliam eshet Uriyah ha-Chitti (“she is Bathsheba daughter of Eliam, the wife of Uriah the Hittite”). The three-part identification is progressively clarifying and progressively damning: her name (Bathsheba), her father’s name (Eliam, one of David’s thirty mighty men per 2 Sam 23:34), and her marital status (wife of Uriah the Hittite — another of David’s thirty mighty men, 2 Sam 23:39). David is told clearly who she is. The information names her married status before anything else; the inquiry has produced the one fact that should stop the king.
4: Vayishlach David mal’akhim vayiqqachenah (“David sent messengers to bring her”). The repeated verb shalach (send) has been the word of David’s royal action throughout the chapter: he sends Joab (v. 1), sends someone to inquire (v. 3), sends messengers for Bathsheba (v. 4), sends for Uriah (v. 6). The sending that expressed chesed in ch. 9-10 has become the sending of exploitation. She came, he lay with her (vatavo elav vayishkav immah). The notation vehi mitqaddesheth mitum’atah (“she had just completed her ritual purification from her monthly uncleanness”) — confirming she is not already pregnant; the resulting pregnancy is David’s.
5: Vathar ha-ishshah vatishtlach vattagged leDavid vatomer harah anokhi (“the woman became pregnant, and she sent word to David: ‘I am pregnant.’”). The terseness of the message — three Hebrew words (harah anokhi, “pregnant I am”) plus the verb — is the most economical sentence of crisis in the entire chapter. Bathsheba does not plead, demand, or accuse. She simply reports. The implication of the report is everything: the king must act. The narrative does not give Bathsheba’s interior state; her agency is limited to this three-word communication.
6: David sent to Joab: shalach eilai et-Uriyah ha-Chitti (“Send me Uriah the Hittite”). So Joab sent Uriah to David. The cover-up plan begins. Uriah’s name means “YHWH is my light” — the faithful soldier with the theophoric name will be manipulated and ultimately murdered by the king whose covenant loyalty was the theme of the preceding chapters. Uriah the Hittite is a non-Israelite serving in Israel’s army with greater covenant fidelity than Israel’s king will demonstrate.
7: When Uriah arrived, David asked about Joab’s welfare (bishalom Yo’av), the troops’ welfare (bishlom ha-am), and the war’s progress (bishlom ha-milchamah). The triple shalom inquiry is a standard greeting ritual but also a cover for the real purpose of the meeting. David is performing normalcy — as if calling a trusted soldier in for a campaign briefing. The word shalom that echoes through these verses will be notably absent from what follows.
8: David’s instruction: red le-veitekha urchatz raglekha (“go home and rest”, literally “go down to your house and wash your feet”). Washing feet was the first act after returning from a journey. David sent a gift (mas’et ha-melekh, a royal present) after him — a bribe intended to encourage domestic ease. The foot-washing invitation is euphemistic for returning to conjugal relations with his wife; the gift is an additional inducement. Both are attempts to create the paternity fiction without Uriah’s knowledge.
9: But Uriah slept at the entrance of the palace with all the king’s other servants (vayishkav Uriyah petach beit ha-melekh im kol-avdei adonav). He did not go home. The refusal is unexplained — did Uriah suspect? Was he simply observing campaign protocol? The narrative leaves it ambiguous. The word velo yared el-beito (“he did not go home”) is the hinge on which the cover-up plan fails. Uriah’s military fidelity will prove unbreakable through direct instruction (v. 11), through drunkenness (v. 13), and ultimately through death.
10: David, informed of Uriah’s refusal, asked: halo min-ha-derekh atah ba madua lo yeradta el-beitekha (“you have just come back from a long journey — why did you not go home?”). The question is disingenuous; David knows exactly why he wants Uriah to go home. The phrase min-ha-derekh atah ba (you have come from the road/journey) is both a factual acknowledgment and an invitation: after the journey, the natural thing is domestic rest.
11: Uriah’s answer is the moral center of the chapter: ha-aron ve-Yisra’el veYehudah yoshevim ba-sukkot va-adoni Yo’av ve-avdei adoni al-penei ha-sadeh chonin va-ani avo el-beiti le-ekhol veli-shtot ule-shkhav im-ishti chayyekha vekhay napshekha im-e’eseh et ha-davar hazeh (“the ark and Israel and Judah are staying in tents, and my lord Joab and your servants are camped in the open country. Should I then go home to eat and drink and sleep with my wife?”). Uriah’s refusal is grounded in solidarity with the ark, with Israel, and with his fellow soldiers. The mention of the ark first — in the field, vulnerable, without the king who should be with it — is an indirect rebuke of David’s absence. The oath formula chayyekha vekhay napshekha (as you live and as your soul lives) is the king’s life invoked as the witness to Uriah’s vow.
12: David’s response: shev poh gam-ha-yom umachar ashalechekha (“stay here today as well, and tomorrow I will let you go”). The promise of release tomorrow is false — David’s actual plan is forming. The extra day in Jerusalem is a second opportunity to get Uriah home. Uriah stayed that day and the next in Jerusalem, still not going home. The repetition of his refusal (lo rad el-beito, “he did not go home”) emphasizes that Uriah is consistently refusing what David offers, not from stubbornness but from principle.
13: David invited him to eat and drink (vayiqra-lo David vayokhal lefanav vayisht) and made him drunk (vayeshakkerehu). But in the evening Uriah went out to sleep among the king’s servants, still not going home. The verb shakhar (make drunk, piel) appears in Gen 43:34 for Benjamin receiving five times as much food — festive excess. David’s drunkenness strategy assumes that inhibitions lowered by wine will overcome Uriah’s principled abstention. The strategy fails: drunk or sober, Uriah maintains his soldiers’ solidarity. The Hittite soldier outperforms the Israelite king in covenant loyalty.
14: In the morning David wrote a letter to Joab (vayikhtov David sepher el Yo’av) and sent it in Uriah’s hand (vayishlach beyad Uriyah). The terrible irony is explicit in the Hebrew: the man carries his own death warrant. Sepher (letter/written document) appears rarely in the Hebrew Bible; this is one of the earliest mentions of a written royal letter used for administrative purposes. Uriah’s illiteracy — or his trust in David — is what makes him the unwitting bearer of the letter that orders his death.
16: While Joab had the city under siege (vishmor Yo’av el-ha-ir), he stationed Uriah at the point where he knew the strongest defenders were (el ha-maqom asher yada’ ki anshei chayil sham). Joab’s execution of the plan is careful: he doesn’t simply order Uriah to run at a wall, but places him where experienced soldiers know the defenders are strongest. The exposure looks tactical rather than deliberate. The verb yada’ (knew) for Joab’s tactical knowledge echoes the “knowing” throughout the chapter: David knew who Bathsheba was (v. 3-4), Joab knows where the danger is (v. 16), YHWH knows what David has done (v. 27).
17: The city’s men sallied out (vayetz’u anshe ha-ir vayillachamu et Yo’av) and attacked Joab’s forces. Some of David’s servants fell, and Uriah the Hittite died too (vayamot gam Uriyah ha-Chitti). The placement of gam (also/too) before Uriah’s name is striking: he dies alongside other soldiers, making his death look incidental. The cover-up of murder as acceptable military casualty is the final obscenity of the episode. The phrase gam Uriyah ha-Chitti stands out from the narrative like an accusation.
18: Joab sent a report (vayishlach Yo’av vayaggeid leDavid et kol divrei ha-milchamah) to David about the full course of the battle. The messenger is given elaborate instructions for anticipating David’s anger at the tactical exposure (vv. 19-21). Joab anticipates that David will be angry at the tactical blunder that cost soldiers their lives. The messenger’s instructions to mention Uriah as the final item are a briefing in crisis communication: save the key information for when David’s anger is rising, to neutralize it.
19: Joab’s instruction to the messenger: ke-khalotekha et kol divrei ha-milchamah ledabber el-ha-melekh (“after you have given the king the full account of the battle”). The sequence is deliberate: full battle report first, then Uriah’s death. The messenger will need to manage David’s emotional response to military failure before delivering the information that transforms military failure into success from David’s perspective. The dark comedy of the situation — Joab coaching his messenger to navigate the king’s reactions — reveals how thoroughly the court has been corrupted.
20: Joab anticipates David’s rebuke: madua niggashtem el ha-ir lahelachem halo yeda’atem et asher yorehu me-al ha-chomah (“Why did you go so close to the city? Did you not know they would shoot from the wall?”). The reference to arrows from the wall is the standard siege-warfare caution: city defenders had the height advantage. David’s anticipated criticism is tactically correct and would normally be appropriate military leadership feedback.
21: Joab references the Abimelech incident: mi-hikkah et Avimelekh ben Yerubbeshet halo-ishah hashlikhah alov peleach rekhayim me-al ha-chomah vayamot beThevetz (“Who struck down Abimelech son of Jerubbesheth? Was it not a woman who dropped a millstone on him from the wall at Thebez?”). This references Judg 9:50-55. Yerubbeshet (for Yerubba’al) follows the pattern of substituting boshet (shame) for ba’al in names to avoid the divine epithet. The millstone death at Thebez was a proverbial example of siege-warfare folly. Joab uses this example as the anticipated context for the messenger’s final line: “then say, Your servant Uriah the Hittite is dead also.”
22: The messenger came and told David everything Joab had sent him to report. The brief report of compliance (vayavo ha-mal’akh vayaggeid leDavid et kol asher shalcho Yo’av) mirrors the pattern throughout the chapter where instructions are executed without variation. The narrative does not record the messenger deviating from Joab’s script — in the event, the messenger may have shortened the anticipated exchange by going directly to the key news (v. 23).
23: The messenger’s report to David: the enemy came out against them in the open field (ki gavru aleinu ha-anashim vayetz’u eleinu ha-sadeh), but Israel pushed them back to the city gate (vayihyu-aleinu ad-petach ha-sha’ar). The tactical narrative explains how the forces came to be close enough to the wall to suffer archery fire. The messenger does not wait for David’s anger to develop before delivering the Uriah news (v. 24).
24: The archers shot at your servants from the wall (vayoru ha-moreh el-avadekha me-al ha-chomah), and some of the king’s men died (vayamutu mi-avdei ha-melekh), and your servant Uriah the Hittite is dead too (vegam Uriyah ha-Chitti met). Again the gam (also/too) places Uriah among the others who died, normalizing a murder. The messenger faithfully delivers the information exactly as Joab instructed, ending with Uriah’s death as the key fact.
25: David’s response to the messenger: ko-tomar el Yo’av al-yera’ bedavrekhа kaze vegam-zeh vegam-zeh tokhal ha-cherev chazzeq milchamtekha el-ha-ir uharesah vechazeqehu (“Tell Joab: Do not let this distress you — the sword is no respecter of persons. Press the attack against the city and take it.”). The phrase lo-tova ha-cherev (the sword consumes impartially/does not pick its victims) is a military aphorism — David normalizes the deaths, including Uriah’s, as the cost of war. The consolation is hollow: David is consoling Joab for doing exactly what David requested. The phrase vegam-zeh vegam-zeh (“this one and that one”) makes all deaths equivalent and all deaths acceptable.
26: When Uriah’s wife (eshet Uriyah) heard that her husband was dead, she mourned for him (vattispod al-ba’alah). She is referred to as “Uriah’s wife” even in her widowhood — the narrative maintains her identity in relation to her husband through the mourning period. The mourning (saphad) is a standard death ritual. The period of mourning is unspecified but conventional (cf. Gen 27:41: Isaac’s death mourning period; Num 20:29: thirty days for Aaron). David waits out the mourning period before acting.
27: Vaya’avor ha-ebel vayishlach David vaya’asepehah el-beito vattehi-lo le-ishshah vateled-lo ben (“when the mourning period was over, David sent for her and brought her to his palace. She became his wife and bore him a son”). The cover-up appears complete: a respectable interval observed, a formal marriage, a child born in wedlock. But the final clause demolishes the appearance: vayera’ ha-davar asher asah David be-einei YHWH (“but the thing David had done was evil in the LORD’s sight”). The verdict formula be-einei YHWH (in the eyes/sight of YHWH) is the standard Deuteronomistic evaluative judgment used throughout Kings and Samuel. For the first time in the David narrative, that judgment is categorically negative: ra’ (evil). YHWH’s sight penetrates what human observation cannot; the chapter that David has choreographed to conceal his sin concludes with YHWH seeing it all.
